messages.content:
Why won't my printer start moving after I turn it on?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 22.00 °C
Bed temperature: 21.50 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.10 °C
Amb humidity: 45.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Based on the telemetry, the machine is currently in the Idle phase. The print time is 0.00 minutes, and both the nozzle (22.00 °C) and bed (21.50 °C) temperatures are below the 30.00 °C threshold. All motor currents and accelerations are at 0.00. You must initiate a heating sequence or load a G-code file to begin operations.

How does increasing retraction distance affect stringing on direct drive extruders?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 45.30
Nozzle temperature: 210.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.25 g
Y acceleration: -0.15 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.50 °C
Amb humidity: 40.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.85 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.85 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.60 A
Filament: 1

Increasing retraction distance pulls the filament further up the hotend during travel moves, reducing pressure in the melt zone. For direct drive extruders, optimal values are typically low (0.5 to 2.0 mm). Excessive retraction pulls molten plastic into the cold zone, which can cause partial clogs or heat creep rather than simply resolving stringing.

I'm noticing significant ringing artifacts on the X-axis during high-speed perimeter passes. What does the current kinematic data suggest?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 120.45
Nozzle temperature: 245.00 °C
Bed temperature: 80.00 °C
X acceleration: 1.45 g
Y acceleration: 0.10 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 25.00 °C
Amb humidity: 38.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 1.20 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.50 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.75 A
Filament: 1

The machine is in the Active Printing phase at 120.45 minutes. The nozzle is at 245.00 °C. The telemetry indicates an X acceleration of 1.45 g and an X-axis motor current of 1.20 A, which are quite high. These rapid acceleration shifts, coupled with high current, suggest aggressive Jerk/acceleration settings. Lowering X-axis acceleration in your slicer should mitigate the resonant frequencies causing the mechanical ringing.

What is PLA filament, and what temperature should I print it at?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 180.20
Nozzle temperature: 90.00 °C
Bed temperature: 45.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.50 °C
Amb humidity: 42.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

PLA (Polylactic Acid) is a biodegradable thermoplastic widely used in FDM printing due to its low warping characteristics and general ease of use. It typically prints optimally with a nozzle temperature between 190 °C and 220 °C, and a bed temperature between 50 °C and 60 °C to promote sufficient bed adhesion.

My printer has been sitting for a minute but hasn't started moving or extruding yet. Is there a heater fault?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 1.50
Nozzle temperature: 150.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.00 °C
Amb humidity: 44.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

The machine is currently in the Preheating phase. The print time is 1.50 minutes, and the nozzle temperature is steadily rising, currently at 150.00 °C. Because X/Y/Z accelerations and the filament motor current are at 0.00 A, the printer is simply waiting to reach the target temperature before initiating kinematic movement or extrusion. There is no fault indicated.

The hotend fan is still running loudly even though the print finished a while ago. Is it safe to shut off the main power?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 340.10
Nozzle temperature: 65.00 °C
Bed temperature: 35.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 22.50 °C
Amb humidity: 48.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

The machine is in the Cooling Down phase. At 340.10 minutes, the nozzle is at 65.00 °C and the bed is at 35.00 °C, with all accelerations and motor currents at 0.00. It is not safe to shut off power yet. Doing so before the nozzle drops below 50.00 °C can cause heat creep, potentially leading to internal hotend clogs. Wait for temperatures to fully normalize.

I just started a print, why is the bed heating but the nozzle isn't moving?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 3.20
Nozzle temperature: 25.00 °C
Bed temperature: 55.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.00 °C
Amb humidity: 46.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

The printer is in the Preheating phase at 3.20 minutes. The bed temperature has reached 55.00 °C, while the nozzle remains at ambient 25.00 °C. Firmware typically heats the bed fully before initiating the nozzle heater to prevent oozing and manage power supply load. Accelerations and motor currents remain at 0.00 A because thermal targets are still pending.

I am experiencing under-extrusion mid-print. Could the extruder motor be failing?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 65.80
Nozzle temperature: 210.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.35 g
Y acceleration: 0.20 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.20 °C
Amb humidity: 41.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.85 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.85 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.15 A
Filament: 1

The machine is in the Active Printing phase at 65.80 minutes with optimal temperatures (Nozzle: 210.00 °C). However, the filament motor current is unusually low at 0.15 A while X/Y accelerations are active. This indicates insufficient torque at the extruder stepper, which can cause slipping and under-extrusion. You should check the stepper driver voltage or inspect for mechanical resistance in the filament path.

Can you explain the difference between a PID and Bang-Bang controller for heated beds?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 24.00 °C
Bed temperature: 24.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.00 °C
Amb humidity: 43.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

A Bang-Bang controller operates as a simple binary switch, applying full power until the target temperature is exceeded, then shutting off completely until it drops below a set threshold, causing measurable thermal oscillations. A PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller continuously calculates a variable power output based on the present error, integral of past errors, and rate of change, resulting in highly stable thermal regulation.

The Z-axis seems to be struggling during layer changes. Are there signs of binding?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 88.30
Nozzle temperature: 205.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.10 g
Amb temperature: 24.80 °C
Amb humidity: 39.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 1.45 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

The machine is in the Active Printing phase. The print time is 88.30 minutes with the nozzle at 205.00 °C. The Z-axis motor current is elevated at 1.45 A during a positive Z acceleration of 0.10 g. This high current draw, while X and Y are idle, indicates excessive mechanical resistance. The lead screw is likely binding due to misalignment, lack of lubrication, or a bent rod.

Why isn't the plastic coming out yet? I pressed start a minute ago.

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 1.20
Nozzle temperature: 140.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.50 °C
Amb humidity: 45.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Right now, your printer is in the Preheating phase. Notice how your nozzle temperature is at 140.00 °C? Standard filament needs to be much hotter to melt properly. The filament motor current is safely at 0.00 A because the machine is waiting to reach the final target temperature before trying to push cold, solid plastic. It just needs a little more time to heat up!

I'm hearing a strange clicking noise from the extruder gear while the first layer goes down. What's wrong?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 5.30
Nozzle temperature: 185.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.15 g
Y acceleration: 0.10 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.00 °C
Amb humidity: 42.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 1.20 A
Filament: 1

We are in the Active Printing phase at 5.30 minutes. Let's look at your numbers: your nozzle temperature is at 185.00 °C, which can be a bit cold for smooth flow. Meanwhile, the filament motor current is quite high at 1.20 A. This means the motor is working extra hard to push semi-solid plastic, causing the gear to slip and "click." Try bumping the temperature up by 5 or 10 degrees.

What is the difference between a skirt and a brim when slicing a file?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 22.00 °C
Bed temperature: 22.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 22.50 °C
Amb humidity: 40.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Good question! Since you're asking a general slicer question, we can ignore the telemetry—though I see your machine is safely resting in the Idle phase. A skirt is a loose outline drawn around your print area to prime the nozzle and get the plastic flowing. A brim, however, connects directly to the edges of your first layer to act like a suction cup, helping prevent corners from warping or lifting off the bed.

I'm getting slight layer shifts on the Y-axis when the bed moves quickly. How can I fix this?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 145.10
Nozzle temperature: 215.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.20 g
Y acceleration: 1.10 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 25.10 °C
Amb humidity: 38.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.80 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.40 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.65 A
Filament: 1

At 145.10 minutes, the machine is well into the Active Printing phase. If we check the telemetry, the Y-axis acceleration is demanding a rapid 1.10 g, but the Y-axis motor current is set relatively low at 0.40 A. This imbalance means the motor doesn't have enough torque to handle sudden directional changes, causing it to lose its place. I'd recommend either lowering your Y-axis acceleration in the slicer or slightly increasing the stepper driver current.

Can you explain how to calibrate extruder E-steps?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 23.50 °C
Bed temperature: 23.50 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.80 °C
Amb humidity: 41.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Since this is a maintenance question, we'll ignore the telemetry, though the machine is perfectly safe in the Idle phase right now. Calibrating E-steps ensures your extruder pushes the exact amount of plastic you request. You simply make a mark on your filament 120mm away from the extruder, tell the printer to extrude 100mm, and measure what's left. If there isn't exactly 20mm left over, you use a simple formula to update the firmware!

The print is done, but the fans are still spinning loudly. Can I just unplug the machine now?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 180.50
Nozzle temperature: 85.00 °C
Bed temperature: 40.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.20 °C
Amb humidity: 44.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Your printer is currently in the Cooling Down phase. Even though the print time shows it finished at 180.50 minutes, the nozzle is still hot at 85.00 °C. Those fans are working hard to cool the internal parts of the hotend. If you unplug the printer now, that heat will travel upward and could cause a nasty clog. Let's wait until the nozzle drops below 50.00 °C before powering off.

My X-axis stepper motor is extremely hot to the touch. Is this normal?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 45.20
Nozzle temperature: 210.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.30 g
Y acceleration: -0.15 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.90 °C
Amb humidity: 43.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 1.65 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.80 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.70 A
Filament: 1

Let's check the numbers. The machine is in the Active Printing phase at 45.20 minutes. Your X-axis motor current is reading 1.65 A, which is notably higher than the Y-axis at 0.80 A. While stepper motors are designed to run warm, this high current explains the excess heat. Running it this high long-term could shorten the motor's lifespan. I'd suggest we tune the reference voltage (VREF) down slightly to give the motor a break.

The printer has been preparing for a while, but the bed feels completely cold. What's happening?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 4.10
Nozzle temperature: 195.00 °C
Bed temperature: 24.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.00 °C
Amb humidity: 45.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

We are currently in the Preheating phase at 4.10 minutes. Interestingly, your nozzle is successfully heating up (it's at 195.00 °C), but your bed temperature is sitting at an ambient 24.00 °C. Usually, the bed is programmed to heat first! This tells us the slicer might have been accidentally set to 0 degrees for the bed, or there could be a disconnected wire. Let's verify your slicer settings before digging into the hardware.

How does volumetric flow rate limit my maximum print speed?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 21.00 °C
Bed temperature: 21.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 21.50 °C
Amb humidity: 39.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

This is a great theoretical question! As your machine is currently resting in the Idle phase, we can focus on the concept. Volumetric flow rate is simply the volume of plastic your hotend can successfully melt per second. If you try to command the printer to move faster than the heater block can melt the plastic, the extruder gear will start grinding the cold filament. Think of it as the ultimate speed limit for your printer!

I just loaded a file, but the machine is totally frozen. Nothing is moving!

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 23.00 °C
Bed temperature: 23.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.50 °C
Amb humidity: 46.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Let's look at the data together. The machine is actually in the Idle phase. The print time is 0.00 minutes, and both temperatures are reading at ambient 23.00 °C. Because all the motor currents and accelerations are at exactly 0.00, the printer simply hasn't received the command to start the job yet. Double-check that your memory card is fully inserted and that you actually selected "Print" on the screen!

Why did my printer suddenly stop pushing plastic? The toolhead is still moving around!

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 75.40
Nozzle temperature: 205.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.25 g
Y acceleration: 0.15 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.10 °C
Amb humidity: 40.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.70 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 0

Let's look at the sensor data together. We are in the Active Printing phase at 75.40 minutes. The X and Y motors are actively moving, but notice that the filament sensor reads "0" (empty) and the filament motor current has dropped to 0.00 A. You've simply run out of plastic! The machine shut off the extruder motor to prevent grinding while the buffer finishes the last few kinematic movements.

When should I use a feature like Z-hop during my prints?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 22.50 °C
Bed temperature: 22.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.00 °C
Amb humidity: 43.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Since your machine is safely resting in the Idle phase, we can focus on the theory. Z-hop, or Z-lift, tells the printer to raise the nozzle slightly before traveling across empty space. It is incredibly useful for preventing the hot nozzle from dragging across already-printed sections, which can knock delicate supports over or leave ugly scars on top surfaces.

The bottom of my print is lifting off the glass bed at the corners. Is something wrong with the heat?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 65.20
Nozzle temperature: 210.00 °C
Bed temperature: 35.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.15 g
Y acceleration: 0.20 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 22.80 °C
Amb humidity: 46.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.65 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.70 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.50 A
Filament: 1

You are spotting the symptoms perfectly. We are in the Active Printing phase, but looking at the telemetry, your bed temperature has dropped to 35.00 °C. Standard filaments usually need the bed to stay around 50.00 °C to 60.00 °C for the entire job. Because the bed cooled down mid-print, the plastic shrank and pulled away from the glass, causing the warping you see.

The machine is shaking violently during the solid infill layers. Are my settings too aggressive?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 15.10
Nozzle temperature: 205.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 1.85 g
Y acceleration: -1.75 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.50 °C
Amb humidity: 39.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 1.50 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 1.45 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.80 A
Filament: 1

Yes, they definitely are. We are in the Active Printing phase, and the telemetry reveals extreme directional shifts. The X and Y accelerations are hitting 1.85 g and -1.75 g, drawing a heavy 1.50 A of current to force the motors to change direction that fast. This aggressive momentum transfer causes the violent shaking. I highly recommend cutting your infill acceleration in half within your slicer settings.

The screen says the nozzle is fully heated, so why isn't it starting the first layer yet?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 3.50
Nozzle temperature: 200.00 °C
Bed temperature: 45.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.20 °C
Amb humidity: 41.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

It can be tricky to wait, but the printer is exactly where it should be in the Preheating phase. While your nozzle has successfully reached 200.00 °C, the bed is currently only at 45.00 °C. The machine will keep all motors at 0.00 A and hold position until the bed reaches its final target (usually around 60.00 °C). It just needs a couple more minutes!

Do I really need a special nozzle to print abrasive materials like carbon fiber blends?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 21.50 °C
Bed temperature: 21.50 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 22.00 °C
Amb humidity: 44.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

That is an excellent hardware question. Right now, the machine is in the Idle phase, so we can ignore the data block. Yes, you absolutely need a special nozzle for abrasive filaments. Standard brass nozzles will get chewed up by the tiny carbon fibers in just a few hours, causing the hole to widen and ruin your extrusion width. You'll want to swap to a hardened steel or ruby-tipped nozzle first.

The job finished a few minutes ago. Is it safe for me to grab a brass brush and scrub the nozzle clean right now?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 120.40
Nozzle temperature: 130.00 °C
Bed temperature: 40.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.00 °C
Amb humidity: 42.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Please don't touch it just yet! The machine is currently in the Cooling Down phase. Even though the motors are off and the print is done, the telemetry shows the nozzle temperature is still a searing 130.00 °C. This is more than hot enough to cause severe burns. Wait until the temperature drops below 40.00 °C before doing any manual cleaning or maintenance.

I hear a terrible grinding noise from the extruder, and the plastic looks patchy. Is my temperature too low?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 30.15
Nozzle temperature: 175.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.25 g
Y acceleration: 0.20 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.80 °C
Amb humidity: 45.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.65 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 1.30 A
Filament: 1

You are absolutely correct. We are in the Active Printing phase, but your nozzle temperature is only 175.00 °C. This is generally too cold to melt the plastic fast enough. Looking at the telemetry, the filament motor current is spiking at 1.30 A because it's fighting massive resistance trying to force solid plastic through the nozzle. Bumping the temperature to at least 195.00 °C should stop the grinding immediately.

Why did my extruder motor just turn completely off in the middle of a layer? The head is still moving.

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 90.00
Nozzle temperature: 215.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.30 g
Y acceleration: 0.25 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.50 °C
Amb humidity: 38.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.80 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.70 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Let's diagnose this. The machine is in the Active Printing phase, and the kinematics confirm the X and Y axes are still moving. However, the filament motor current has unexpectedly dropped to 0.00 A, despite filament being present. This usually points to a thermal protection fault on the stepper driver motherboard. The driver likely overheated and shut itself down to prevent a fire, killing power to the extruder.

What is the point of using a raft instead of just printing right on the build plate?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 23.00 °C
Bed temperature: 23.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 23.50 °C
Amb humidity: 41.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Great slicer question! Your printer is in the Idle phase right now, so we can look purely at the concept. A raft prints a thick, disposable grid of plastic underneath your actual object. It acts as a transitional foundation. You typically use a raft if your build plate is slightly warped or uneven, or if the material you are using is notoriously difficult to stick to glass or metal.

Why does the screen show the bed is hot, but the printer hasn't started making my print yet?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 2.50
Nozzle temperature: 24.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 22.00 °C
Amb humidity: 41.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Your machine is in the Preheating phase right now. It is completely normal for the printer to wait like this! Looking at the numbers, your bed temperature has successfully reached 60.00 °C, but the nozzle is still sitting at 24.00 °C. The printer's brain is programmed to heat the bed first, then heat the nozzle, to prevent plastic from oozing out while the bed warms up.

What is PID tuning, and why would I need to run it after changing my hotend?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 23.50 °C
Bed temperature: 23.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.00 °C
Amb humidity: 39.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

Since your machine is safely resting in the Idle phase, we can focus on the theory! PID tuning is a way to teach your printer how to heat up smoothly without overshooting the target temperature. Because a new hotend might have a different metal block or a stronger heater cartridge, the old math won't work anymore. Running a PID tune recalibrates that heating algorithm for perfect stability.

I'm hearing a low humming and grinding noise from the vertical uprights during standard side-to-side moves. Is my gantry too heavy?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 45.10
Nozzle temperature: 210.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.45 g
Y acceleration: 0.30 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g

Grid infill prints straight lines that cross over each other, which is fast to slice but causes the nozzle to physically scrape over the intersections during the print. Gyroid infill prints a continuous 3D wave pattern that never crosses itself. Gyroid provides highly uniform strength in all directions and prevents the nozzle from scraping, making it an excellent choice for functional parts.

i'm printing PETG plastic for the first time, and i notice the cooling fan on the print head is barely spinning compared to when i print PLA. why?

Current of the X axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A

Filament: 1

That is exactly what should happen during the Active Printing phase for PETG! Different materials have very different thermal properties. PLA loves being blasted with cold air so it hardens instantly. PETG, however, needs to stay very hot to chemically bond to the layer underneath it. If the cooling fan ran at 100%, the PETG would cool too quickly, and your final part would have incredibly weak layer adhesion, easily snapping apart like a cracker. The slicer deliberately turns the fan down!

why is it generally better to add more outer wall layers for strength instead of just making the inside 100% solid plastic?

Nozzle temperature: 24.00 °C
Bed temperature: 24.00 °C

Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

This is a fantastic engineering question while your machine is in the Idle phase! It comes down to how physical stress works. When you bend a part, all the tension and compression forces are concentrated almost entirely on the outermost surfaces, while the center of the part experiences almost zero stress. By adding more outer walls (perimeters), you put the plastic exactly where the bending forces are highest, giving you a massively stronger part while using much less plastic than a 100% solid block!

right before it started, a little metal pin dropped down and the nozzle tapped the bed in like 16 different spots. what was it measuring?

That tapping routine was your Auto Bed Leveling (ABL) probe at work! Even high-quality glass or metal beds are never perfectly flat on a microscopic level; they have tiny hills and valleys. During that routine, the Z-axis motor (currently active at 0.50 A) carefully lowered the probe to map the exact physical height of the bed at multiple points. The printer's brain uses those measurements to create a 3D topographic map, allowing the nozzle to subtly rise and fall to perfectly match the contours of your bed while printing.

when the nozzle travels between two different parts of my model, it takes a long curved path to stay inside the printed area instead of just going straight across the open air. why?

You are seeing a clever slicer setting called 'Combing' or 'Avoid Crossing Perimeters'! Notice your filament motor current is at 0.00 A during this move; it isn't extruding, but pressure still exists inside the hot nozzle. If it traveled straight across the open air, that residual pressure would ooze out and create messy strings between your parts. By forcing the nozzle to travel *inside* the perimeter walls of your already-printed part, any oozing gets wiped off internally where it will never be seen!

people keep telling me to run a 'PID tune' since i swapped my hotend out. what does that actually do for the printer?

That is great advice while you have the machine in the Idle phase! Every heater block has a different physical mass and reacts to electricity differently. A PID (Proportional, Integral, Derivative) tune is an automated calibration cycle. The motherboard turns the heater on and off rapidly, monitoring exactly how fast the new metal block heats up and cools down. It uses this data to learn the perfect rhythm of electrical pulses needed to hold your target temperature perfectly flat without swinging too hot or too cold.

That feeling of it being locked is called 'Holding Torque'! Even though the printer isn't actively moving (0 acceleration), you can see from your telemetry that the X, Y, and Z motors are still drawing 0.50 A of electrical current. The motherboard is intentionally energizing the internal magnetic coils to lock the motors firmly in their exact current position. This ensures that when the print resumes, the toolhead hasn't drifted even a fraction of a millimeter out of alignment!

Your thermostat is working perfectly! What you just witnessed is called 'thermal overshoot.' Because the heater cartridge applies full electrical power to warm up the metal block quickly, the heat continues to spread through the metal even after the power is momentarily cut at 200.00 °C. The motherboard expects this slight overshoot during the Preheating phase and uses its PID algorithm to let it naturally cool back down and stabilize exactly at your target.

Your fan is perfectly fine! The slicer software specifically commanded it to stay off. ABS plastic is incredibly sensitive to temperature changes. If the cooling fan were to blow air on the freshly printed ABS, the top layers would shrink and contract much faster than the hot lower layers. This uneven cooling causes severe warping and causes the layers to split apart horizontally. For strong ABS prints, you actually want the plastic to cool down as slowly and naturally as possible!

That is a great foundational question while the machine is in the Idle phase! 'G-code' is literally just the language the printer speaks. A 3D model is just a picture, but the printer only understands math. Your slicer software translates that 3D model into millions of lines of text instructions (G-code). why does my bed temperature automatically drop from 65 to 60 after the first few layers finish printing?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 15.30
Nozzle temperature: 205.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.15 g
Y acceleration: 0.15 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.20 °C
Amb humidity: 41.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.50 A
Filament: 1

That temperature drop is a very clever setting designed to give you the best of both worlds! During the first few layers, a hotter bed (65.00 °C) makes the plastic stickier, ensuring perfect adhesion to the glass so the print doesn't fail. However, keeping the bed that hot for the entire print keeps the bottom layers too soft, causing them to bulge outward under the weight of the part. Dropping it to 60.00 °C solidifies the foundation while maintaining enough heat to keep it stuck.

the extruder gear is twitching back and forth really fast right as the nozzle hits the sharp corners. is it stuttering?

It's not stuttering; you are watching an advanced firmware feature called 'Linear Advance' (or Pressure Advance) in action! Because you are printing at a high acceleration (1.50 g), a lot of pressure builds up inside the hot nozzle. When the toolhead slows down to turn a sharp corner, that pressure would normally ooze out, causing a bulging, rounded corner. The extruder motor rapidly twitches back to relieve that pressure right *before* the corner, giving you perfectly crisp, sharp edges!

my printer moves the entire heavy glass bed back and forth for the Y-axis, but my friend's printer only moves the tiny nozzle around. what's the difference?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 24.00 °C
Bed temperature: 24.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g

Amb humidity: 42.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

That is an excellent mechanical design question! Your printer is what's called a 'Cartesian 'bed-slinger'.' It is simpler, cheaper to build, and very reliable. The tradeoff is that throwing that heavy glass bed back and forth limits how fast you can print before vibrations ruin the quality. Your friend likely has a 'CoreXY' printer, where the bed only moves down slowly, and the lightweight nozzle does all the fast X and Y movements, allowing for incredibly high printing speeds!

i'm looking at my menu and there is a setting called 'Jerk'. why would a printer need a setting named that?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 23.50 °C
Bed temperature: 23.50 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.00 °C
Amb humidity: 41.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

In physics, 'Jerk' is the actual scientific term for the rate of change of acceleration! In 3D printing terms, Jerk dictates the maximum instantaneous speed jump the toolhead is allowed to make from a complete dead stop, without smoothly ramping up via acceleration. A higher Jerk setting makes the printer take sharp corners much faster and reduces bulging, but if you set it too high, the sudden violent physical jump will cause the motors to skip steps and shift your layers.

the part cooling fan just keeps speeding up and slowing down randomly. it'll be quiet, then suddenly roar when it goes over an overhang. how does it know?

Filament: 1

You are witnessing Dynamic Fan Control! Your slicer software is incredibly smart. It knows that standard straight walls don't need maximum cooling, so it runs the fan quietly to keep layer adhesion strong. However, when it mathematically calculates that it is about to print an overhang in mid-air, it inserts special G-code to instantly ramp the fan to 100%. This sudden blast of cold air freezes the plastic mid-air before gravity pulls it down, and then quiets back down for the straight walls.

i upgraded my motherboard and now the printer is completely silent! it used to sound like a loud robot. what actually changed?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 15.00
Nozzle temperature: 205.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.15 g
Y acceleration: 0.15 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g

Amb humidity: 39.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.50 A
Filament: 1

The silence comes from the new 'Stepper Drivers' on your upgraded motherboard! Older drivers send electrical current to the motors in harsh, blocky, square waves, which makes the metal coils violently vibrate and sing that classic robotic song. Your new board uses advanced drivers (like Trinamic TMC drivers) that chop the electricity into incredibly smooth, high-frequency sine waves. It moves the motors with such precision and fluidity that the physical vibration is entirely eliminated!

Filament: 1

Bed leveling didn't turn off; it gracefully faded out! This is a brilliant firmware feature called 'Fade Height'. Your glass bed has microscopic hills and valleys, and the printer compensates for them on the bottom layers to ensure the plastic sticks perfectly. However, if it copied those hills all the way up, the top of your print would be permanently warped. By gradually reducing the compensation over the first few millimeters, the firmware ensures the top of your object finishes perfectly flat and dimensionally accurate.

Your infill is generating exactly as intended; you have discovered 'Lightning Infill'! Unlike traditional grid patterns that fill the entire interior from the bottom up, Lightning infill is highly optimized. It mathematically calculates exactly where the top 'roof' of your model needs support. It leaves the lower sections completely hollow to save massive amounts of plastic and print time, and only generates those branching, lightning-like webs right near the top to hold up the ceiling.

That thin tube is a slicer feature called a 'Draft Shield', and it is crucial for materials like ABS! Look at your ambient room temperature—it is a chilly 20.00 °C, but your bed is at 100.00 °C. If that cold room air hit your hot ABS part, it would shrink violently and warp. The printer builds that thin disposable wall around the part to trap the radiant heat from the bed, creating a warm, stable micro-climate that prevents your part from cracking as it prints.

the supports look super thin and weak, but right before it touches the actual model, it prints a solid flat roof on top of them. why the sudden change in density?

Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.65 A
Filament: 1

That solid roof is called a 'Support Interface Layer', and it provides the best of both worlds! The main vertical body of the support structure is printed with very low density to save you plastic, time, and make it easy to crush and remove. However, if the printer tried to extrude the final model directly onto a sparse grid, the plastic would sag into the gaps. The solid interface layer gives your actual model a perfectly flat, clean ceiling to print on, while still breaking away easily later.

right before the toolhead jumps to a new spot, it kind of wipes the nozzle back into the wall it just printed while pulling the plastic back. what is the point of the wipe?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 35.00
Nozzle temperature: 200.00 °C
Bed temperature: 60.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.20 g
Y acceleration: 0.20 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.00 °C
Amb humidity: 42.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.60 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.60 A
Filament: 1

That motion is an advanced anti-stringing technique called 'Wipe While Retracting'. Even when the extruder gear pulls the filament backward (retraction), there is still a tiny droplet of pressurized, melted plastic hanging right on the tip of the nozzle. If the toolhead just jumped into the open air, that droplet would drag and create a hairy string. By wiping the nozzle against the inside of the wall before jumping, the printer physically smears that leftover droplet safely into the part where it will never be seen.

what is the point of changing the 'flow rate' percentage on the printer screen if the slicer software already calculated exactly how much plastic to push?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 23.50 °C
Bed temperature: 23.50 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g

Z acceleration: 0.00 g

Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

The flow rate setting is your manual override for real-world manufacturing tolerances! Your slicer does perfect math, assuming your spool of plastic is exactly 1.75mm thick. However, in reality, cheaper spools might actually measure 1.72mm or 1.78mm thick. If the filament is slightly too thick, the printer will push out too much material, causing a rough surface. The flow rate percentage allows you to make live, on-the-fly micro-adjustments to compensate for the physical spool without having to go back to the computer and re-slice the entire file.

the top layer on this circular cylinder is being drawn in perfectly shrinking circles instead of the usual diagonal lines. it looks awesome, what setting did that?

You are witnessing the 'Concentric' top/bottom pattern setting! Most slicers default to a 'Lines' or 'Zig-Zag' pattern, which fills the surface by moving back and forth diagonally. The Concentric setting tells the printer to look at the exact shape of the outer walls, and then continuously trace that exact shape inward, smaller and smaller, until it meets in the very center. It provides an incredibly smooth, visually pleasing aesthetic, especially for round parts like cylinders or domes.

i ran my file through an 'arc welder' plugin and now the file size is way smaller, and the printer moves so much smoother on curves. how did it shrink the file?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.00
Nozzle temperature: 24.00 °C
Bed temperature: 24.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g

Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

That plugin changes how the printer understands geometry! Standard slicers cannot draw true curves; they fake a curve by breaking it into hundreds of tiny, microscopic straight lines. That means hundreds of lines of G-code, which creates a huge file size and forces the printer's brain to process commands so fast that it sometimes stutters. Arc Welder converts all those tiny lines into a single, elegant mathematical G2 or G3 Arc command. The file size plummets, and the motherboard simply calculates a perfectly smooth continuous sweep.

when i home the printer, there are no physical clicky switches on the x or y axis. the print head just slams into the metal frame and stops. is that actually safe?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.10
Nozzle temperature: 24.00 °C
Bed temperature: 24.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.05 g
Y acceleration: 0.05 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g
Amb temperature: 24.50 °C
Amb humidity: 41.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.30 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.30 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

It is completely safe, and it is actually a highly advanced feature called 'Sensorless Homing'! Instead of using cheap mechanical switches that can break over time, your printer uses incredibly smart stepper motor drivers on the motherboard. When the toolhead gently bumps into the metal frame, the physical resistance causes a sudden spike in electrical current inside the motor. The motherboard instantly detects that microsecond electrical spike, realizes it hit the edge, and safely registers that exact spot as the zero coordinate.

Yes, you can! Printers can usually handle overhangs up to about 45 degrees without any help at all, because each layer only slightly overlaps the one below it. If you orient your model so the steepest parts are facing upward, you can often avoid supports entirely. Additionally, increasing your part-cooling fan speed can help solidify the plastic faster, allowing you to print steeper angles (sometimes up to 60 or 70 degrees) without needing any support structures at all!

Every time I turn on my printer, the fan in the power supply immediately kicks on and stays on, but the print head fans don't start until later. Why the difference?

Process parameters:
Print time in minutes: 0.10
Nozzle temperature: 24.00 °C
Bed temperature: 24.00 °C
X acceleration: 0.00 g
Y acceleration: 0.00 g
Z acceleration: 0.00 g

Amb humidity: 40.00 %
Current of the X axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Y axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the Z axis motor: 0.00 A
Current of the filament motor: 0.00 A
Filament: 1

That is because they serve completely different purposes. The power supply fan is wired directly into the main power circuit, so it runs whenever the machine is on to prevent the electronics from overheating. The print head fans are managed by the motherboard. One is a 'hotend' fan, which should run constantly to stop heat creep, and the other is a 'part-cooling' fan, which only turns on during the actual printing process to harden the plastic. They are two entirely different systems!

It is completely normal to be cautious! Modern printers have many built-in safety features like thermal runaway protection, but it's always best to be sensible. For an eight-hour print, ensure the printer is on a flat, fire-resistant surface, keep it away from flammable curtains or clutter, and consider using a smoke detector in the same room. Many users also use a cheap webcam to monitor the progress remotely, so you can stop the print instantly from your phone if you see anything go wrong.

That circular move is actually a 'purge' or 'wipe' move often used in dual-extrusion printers or specialized settings. It ensures that the nozzle is clean and the pressure is perfectly balanced before starting the next layer. By circling the model, the printer can wipe away any tiny droplets of plastic that might have formed, ensuring that when the nozzle finally moves onto the model, it starts with a clean, perfectly pressurized line, preventing those ugly blobs that often happen at the start of a layer.
